results.possibleIdentity: George Adams (born Georg Teltscher, 1904–1983) was an Austrian-born graphic designer and educator who was a former student at the Bauhaus under masters such as Wassily Kandinsky, Paul Klee, and Walter Gropius. After immigrating to London in 1938, he became a prominent figure in the British design community, teaching at the London College of Printing and serving as a consultant for the major Bauhaus retrospective at the Royal Academy in 1968. He was actively involved in the intersection of art and technology during the late 1960s and early 1970s, having been invited by his Bauhaus associates to take on design commissions at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) starting in 1957. Between 1973 and 1977, he lived in Nigeria, where he was invited to establish the Art Department at the University of Nigeria in Nsukka. His work, which included set designs for 'Das Mechanische Ballett' and numerous book designs for publishers like Thames & Hudson, is held in the collections of the British Museum and the Victoria and Albert Museum.

George Adams was a young architect active in the late 1960s who is best known for his role in the design of the Central Laboratory building (now known as Wilson Hall) at Fermilab in Batavia, Illinois. In 1967, he was recruited by Robert R. Wilson, the founding director of Fermilab and a noted physicist and sculptor, to submit a design for the lab's main building. Adams' winning entry, a truncated cone structure with a large domed atrium, was selected for its ability to satisfy both the technical requirements of the physics facility and Wilson's aesthetic vision of the laboratory as a work of art. This project reflected the core philosophy of contemporary organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), emphasizing the deep connection between science, engineering, and sculpture.

George Adams is a prominent New York art dealer and owner of the George Adams Gallery (originally the Allan Frumkin Gallery). While he is primarily known as a gallerist rather than a practicing artist, his professional identity is inextricably linked to the art of the 1960s and 1970s. He became associated with the Allan Frumkin Gallery in 1980, eventually forming the Frumkin/Adams Gallery in 1988 before taking full ownership. The gallery has long specialized in representing artists who were active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, including Robert Arneson, Joan Brown, and Peter Saul, many of whom explored the boundaries of figuration and social commentary during the era when Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) was most active.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Tucker (active late 1960s – 1970s) was an American art educator and artist who contributed to the study and promotion of technological art movements. In 1977, he was an art teacher at Springbrook High School in Maryland and was a key contributor to the development of the 'Aesthetic Education' curriculum for Montgomery County Public Schools. This program specifically featured the 'Experiments in Art and Technology' (E.A.T.) organization and examined the works of artists like Robert Rauschenberg and Nam June Paik. His work in this period focused on process-oriented art and the collaborative relationship between artists and engineers.

Robert J. Tucker III (BFA 1964, MA 1965) is a 20th-century American artist and printmaker, and Professor Emeritus of Art at Birmingham-Southern College. He was active as a working artist and educator from the mid-1960s through the 1970s and beyond. His artistic output includes screenprints, drawings, and sculpture, often characterized by geometric compositions. Notable works from the early 1970s include 'Geometric Tablet' (1970) and 'Geometric Composition' (1975). He is recognized as a master printmaker and was an influential figure in the Alabama art community.

Robert Tucker (died 2016) was a prominent Tony-nominated choreographer and performer active in theater and film during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He made his Broadway choreographic debut in 1968 with 'Sweet Potato' and earned a Tony Award nomination for his work on the 1974 musical 'Shenandoah'. Throughout the 1960s, he served as an assistant to legendary choreographers Jerome Robbins and Bob Fosse on major productions such as 'Gypsy' and 'Bells Are Ringing'.

results.possibleIdentity: Alfred J. Wyatt (1926–2012) was a prominent fine arts photographer who served as the Director of the Photographic Department at the Philadelphia Museum of Art (PMA) from 1955 to 1976. He was active during the late 1960s and early 1970s, documenting major art collections and exhibitions. His work is credited in numerous high-profile art publications and catalogs from that era, such as "The Cubist Epoch" (1970). Following his tenure at the PMA, he served as the Supervisor of the Photographic Services Department at West Point Military Academy until his retirement in 1994. Given the association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which frequently collaborated with museum professionals and technical specialists to document and promote new media, his role as a technical expert in a major museum's photography department aligns with the organization's goals.

Alfred "Al" Wyatt (died 1985) was an artist and painter based in Brooklyn, New York, who was active during the 1960s and 1970s. He was known for producing large-scale figurative and abstract portrait oil paintings, sometimes executed on unconventional materials such as large cardboard tubes. He was a part of the Brooklyn art scene, specifically in the Park Slope neighborhood, and frequently exhibited his work at the Belanthi Gallery in Brooklyn Heights. His life and career are documented by his widow, the artist Bernette Rudolph. He died in 1985 just before the opening of a retrospective exhibition of his work. His location in New York City during the peak years of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization makes him a plausible candidate for membership or association with the group.

results.possibleIdentity: James D. Campbell is a prominent Canadian art critic, curator, and historian who has written extensively on the intersection of art, science, and technology. He is notably associated with the legacy of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), having curated the exhibition "9 Evenings Reconsidered: Art, Theatre, and Engineering" (2007), which revisited the landmark 1966 E.A.T. event. His work often explores the collaboration between artists and engineers, and he has authored numerous books and catalogs on contemporary artists, including "The Asymmetric Vision" (1989), which features his dialogues with artists active since the late 1960s.

James "Jimmy" Campbell (1945–2020) was an artist and activist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a central figure in the counterculture scenes of San Francisco's Haight-Ashbury and New York's Chelsea. He is best known in the art world for designing elaborate stage sets for The Cockettes, an avant-garde psychedelic theater group based in San Francisco between 1969 and 1972. His artistic career spanned several decades and included set design, card design, and political activism, often collaborating with other creative figures of the era.

Jim Campbell (born 1956) is a contemporary artist based in San Francisco who is widely recognized for his work with electronic media, particularly LED light installations that explore human perception and low-resolution imagery. While he began his artistic career in filmmaking and transitioned to electronic sculpture in the late 1980s, his work is frequently linked to the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He was a featured artist in the 2015 exhibition "NEAT: New Experiments in Art and Technology" at the Contemporary Jewish Museum, which was explicitly designed as a modern reimagining of the original E.A.T. collaborations.

results.possibleIdentity: Joel Mason is an American artist, graphic designer, and educator who was active in the New York art and design scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is currently Professor Emeritus in the Department of Communication Design at New York City College of Technology (CUNY). Mason is notably associated with public art and technological projects; in 1969, he proposed the "MoonSite" event, which suggested that the major broadcast networks televise the Apollo 11 moon landing on giant screens in Central Park's Sheep Meadow. His poster for this event, titled "MoonSite," was selected for the Smithsonian Institution's exhibition "Images of an Era: The American Poster 1945–1975." From 1970 to 1973, Mason served as the Director of Graphic Design for the New York City Parks, Recreation & Cultural Affairs Administration (PRCA), where he designed "The Where Book," a guide to city park facilities. His work has been exhibited at the American Institute of Graphic Arts (AIGA) Gallery ("The Ubiquitous Sign," 1970) and various CUNY faculty exhibitions since 1979. His interdisciplinary approach to using technology and media in public space aligns with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization active in New York during the same period.

results.possibleIdentity: Helmut Amann is a contemporary artist based in New York City, whose career began in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in Stuttgart, Germany, he immigrated to North America and attended Cass Technical High School before earning a BFA from the Pratt Institute in 1970. His work primarily consists of abstract painting, shaped canvases, and three-dimensional constructions. He is known for employing geometric illusion and a meticulous focus on color, form, and shadow. His artistic practice, which emphasizes the fusion of divergent elements into three-dimensional reliefs, aligns with the period of technological and artistic experimentation of the early 1970s in New York, a time when the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) was highly active.

Hermann Amann (born 1934) is a French-German Postwar and Contemporary painter active since the 1960s. He is noted for his association with early computer art pioneers and the broader tech-art movement in Europe. In 1972, he founded the artistic movement "Jacob ou la Persuasion," which included figures such as computer art pioneer Manfred Mohr and Lettrist artists François Dufrêne and Gil Joseph Wolman. His work often explores "sens et matière" (sense and matter) and pigments, sometimes using acrylic and gouache on various supports. Given his collaboration with early electronic and computer artists during the early 1970s, he shares the interdisciplinary spirit of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Hans Amann is a German engineer and academic associated with the Technical University of Berlin (TU Berlin). Since the late 1960s and 1970s, he has been a prominent figure in maritime technology and deep-sea mining engineering. While primarily a scientist and engineer, the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization was specifically designed to pair engineers with artists to create collaborative projects. Amann's expertise in high-tech sampling and experimental data gathering in the deep sea reflects the type of technical expertise E.A.T. sought to integrate into the art world during its peak years.

results.possibleIdentity: Ken Marsh (born Kenneth Marsh) is a pioneering American video artist, painter, and author who was prominently active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Originally trained as a painter and printmaker at Cooper Union, he began his career working with light shows and kinetic events before becoming a central figure in the nascent video art movement in New York City. In 1970, he co-founded the People's Video Theatre (PVT) with Elliot Glass, a collective that utilized the newly available portapak technology for community activism and "video mediation," a process of recording and playing back local interviews to foster social dialogue. Marsh was closely associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and was among the artists selected for the E.A.T.-led Pepsi Pavilion project at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. In 1971, he moved to Woodstock, New York, where he established Woodstock Community Video (WCV), serving as its director and facilitating residencies for other artists, including Gary Hill. He authored several influential texts on media technology, most notably "Independent Video" (1974) and "The Way the New Technology Works" (1982), and was a contributor to the seminal video publication "Radical Software." In later years, he returned to fine arts practice, specializing in painting and digital photography.

results.possibleIdentity: Peter Aaron is a prominent architectural photographer who began his career in the late 1960s. A 1968 graduate of Bard College, he worked initially as a fashion and advertising photographer in New York City during the late 1960s and early 1970s before transitioning to architectural photography. He trained under the legendary architectural photographer Ezra Stoller and eventually became a principal at Esto Photographics. His association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is established through his extensive documentation of their projects; for example, he is credited with photographing the iconic mirrored dome used in the 1970 Pepsi Pavilion at the Osaka World's Fair—a seminal E.A.T. collaboration involving artists like Robert Whitman and Robert Rauschenberg. Throughout the 1970s and beyond, his work documented the intersection of high-tech industrial design and architecture, including the early projects of Joe D'Urso, Michael Graves, and Robert A.M. Stern. He is a member of the American Institute of Architects (AIA).

Peter Aaron is a writer, musician, and the long-time arts and music editor for Chronogram magazine. While he is deeply connected to the New York arts scene, his primary activity as a public figure—including his role as the frontman for the influential garage-noise band the Chrome Cranks—began in the late 1980s. He has authored several books on music history, including 'The Band FAQ' and 'If You Like the Ramones...', and has been active in the Hudson Valley arts community for over 40 years. He is known for promoting early concerts by bands such as Nirvana and the Flaming Lips in the 1980s. Although he writes extensively on 1960s and 70s art and music history, he is distinct from the architectural photographer of the same name who was professionally active during the E.A.T. era.

results.possibleIdentity: Dolores H. Schapiro (1930–2022), known as "Dilly," was a New York-based designer and arts patron active during the late 1960s and 1970s. She earned a bachelor's degree in English from Smith College in 1951 and later pursued a degree in environmental design from the Parsons School of Design (a division of The New School), graduating in 1973. She also attended the Columbia University School of Architecture. During the 1970s, she worked for various New York architectural firms and was a staff member of the West Side Highway Project, a major urban infrastructure initiative in Manhattan. Schapiro was a prominent arts enthusiast and a long-time patron of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the Metropolitan Museum of Art. Her background in environmental design and her professional ties to the New York architectural and design community during the early 1970s align with the interdisciplinary and collaborative goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

results.possibleIdentity: Martin Craig (1906–1996) was an American sculptor and educator active in the New York art scene during the mid-to-late 20th century. He was a member of the American Abstract Artists and is known for his work in sculpture, which he discussed in his 1961 article "Notes on Sculpture in a Mad Society" published in Art News. Archival records from the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) indicate a connection to Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), listing him alongside correspondence with E.A.T. founder Billy Klüver. His work from the 1960s and 1970s often explored the boundaries of sculpture and environmental art. He also taught at New York University.

Michael Craig-Martin (born 1941) is a highly influential Irish-born conceptual artist and painter who became prominent in the late 1960s. After studying at Yale University under Josef Albers, he moved to London in 1966. His early work involved minimalist constructions and ready-made objects, such as his 1970 piece "On the Table." He is most famous for the 1973 conceptual work "An Oak Tree." His practice frequently intersects with technology and the investigation of everyday objects through precise line drawings and vibrant colors. As a professor at Goldsmiths, he mentored the Young British Artists (YBAs) generation. While he is most commonly referred to by his full name, some archival and auction records refer to him as M. Craig-Martin or M. Craig.
